U.S. Agricultural Water Efficiency Analysis

Agricultural Water Efficiency

Human Factors
−r+
Crop DiversityCrop Diversity (HHI)
Herfindahl-Hirschman Index. Lower HHI = more diverse crops. Diversification reduces single-crop risk and improves water efficiency.

r=-0.50 (negative) means higher diversity → higher efficiency.
High-Water Crop ShareHigh-Water Crop Share
Value share of high-irrigation crops (rice, cotton). Higher share → more water per unit value → lower efficiency.

r=-0.28, one of the most actionable human factors.
Farm SizeFarm Size (acres)
County average farm size. Larger farms may benefit from scale but also tend toward less precise irrigation.

r=-0.28, larger farms correlate with lower efficiency.
Poverty RatePoverty Rate (%)
Share of population in poverty. Low-income counties lack investment in water-saving equipment and precision irrigation.

r=-0.22: socioeconomic conditions constrain agricultural modernization.
Income LevelIncome Level (Median Household)
County median household income ($). Higher income enables investment in precision irrigation.

r=+0.16, positively correlated with water efficiency.
Center Pivot CoverageCenter Pivot Coverage
Share of area with center-pivot irrigation. Precision irrigation significantly reduces evaporation loss.

Not significant: correlation is unstable nationally, likely masked by regional variation, but remains an important intervention.
Climate Factors
Precip DeficitPrecip Deficit (in/yr)
ETo minus precipitation. Used as climate control variable — higher deficit means greater irrigation demand.

Climate control: accounts for natural conditions, not an actionable target.
—
Drought IntensityDrought Intensity
Composite drought stress index. Arid regions have higher irrigation demand and water consumption.

r=-0.35, climate control variable, not actionable.
ElevationElevation (ft)
County average elevation. Higher elevations have lower temperatures and evapotranspiration, but also limited agricultural conditions.

r=-0.30, topographic control variable.
Soil Factors
Soil AWCSoil Water-Holding Capacity (AWC)
Available water capacity, 0-50cm. Good soil retention reduces irrigation frequency.

r=+0.44, strongest positive correlation with water efficiency.
Clay ContentClay Content (%)
Share of clay in soil. Affects water retention and drainage.

r=+0.10: weak positive correlation, soil control variable.
Organic MatterOrganic Matter %
Soil organic matter percentage. More organic matter → better soil structure → improved water retention.

Not significant: linear correlation with efficiency is unstable nationally.
Human Factors
−r+
Crop DiversityCrop Diversity (HHI)
Herfindahl-Hirschman Index. Lower HHI = more diverse crops. Diversification reduces single-crop risk and improves water efficiency.

r=-0.50 (negative) means higher diversity → higher efficiency.
High-Water Crop ShareHigh-Water Crop Share
Value share of high-irrigation crops (rice, cotton). Higher share → more water per unit value → lower efficiency.

r=-0.28, one of the most actionable human factors.
Farm SizeFarm Size (acres)
County average farm size. Larger farms may benefit from scale but also tend toward less precise irrigation.

r=-0.28, larger farms correlate with lower efficiency.
Poverty RatePoverty Rate (%)
Share of population in poverty. Low-income counties lack investment in water-saving equipment and precision irrigation.

r=-0.22: socioeconomic conditions constrain agricultural modernization.
Income LevelIncome Level (Median Household)
County median household income ($). Higher income enables investment in precision irrigation.

r=+0.16, positively correlated with water efficiency.
Center Pivot CoverageCenter Pivot Coverage
Share of area with center-pivot irrigation. Precision irrigation significantly reduces evaporation loss.

Not significant: correlation is unstable nationally, likely masked by regional variation, but remains an important intervention.
SHAP Beeswarm — Click to enlarge
K-means clustering (k=4) categorizes 1,407 counties by climate, soil & human factors. Click any county on the map to see its cluster.
High-Efficiency Grain Belt
675 counties · HHI avg 0.47 · efficiency: High
Diversified crops, favorable climate — highest water efficiency nationally. Mainly Midwest grain belt.
Resource-Constrained
298 counties · poverty avg 19% · efficiency: Low
High poverty rate, low diversity, limited capital for water-saving tech — greatest improvement potential.
Extreme-Arid Low-Efficiency
309 counties · drought intensity 0.78 · large farms
Extreme drought, large farms, high-water crops concentrated — highest water stress, lowest efficiency.
Arid Large-Farm
125 counties · avg farm size 2543 ac
Very large farms, high elevation (avg 3,440 ft) — scale benefits offset by drought pressure, below-average efficiency.

Factor Analysis
Crop diversity is the single largest factor separating the highest and lowest efficiency counties.
SHAP Contributions — Best vs. Worst County
The same factor has very different effects across regions — interventions should be geographically targeted.
